label: Addressing Harmful Algal Blooms in Lake Erie

comments.text: The Raisin River watershed covers part of southeast Michigan. It's filled with sediment and fertilizer from surface runoff because most farmers won't create riparian buffers. The river empties into Lake Erie, where eutrophication causes toxic algal blooms in summer. Blissfield Michigan, 24 Jun 2025.

Wanna see something wild? 🧪 On March 15, 2020, the ESA's Copernicus Sentinel-3 captured a series of images of The Great Lakes from orbit (stitched together here) that show the extent of algal blooms, high turbidity, and other water quality issues, ESPECIALLY for Lake Erie. 😬

In the fight against toxic algae blooms, researchers are giving natural and synthetic clay a shot. The results will help inform scientists about whether the treatment methods could be used on a larger scale, such as Lake Erie, where harmful algal blooms are a costly annual problem.

Catch up on the latest podcast about my remote sensing work on Harmful Algal Blooms in South Africa & Lake Erie. As a #NationalGeographic Explorer, I worked with colleagues from the College of Charleston & Wits University in South Africa on HABS in Vaal Dam 🧪 americaview.podbean.com/e/toledo-was...

Overloading lakes, rivers, & coastal oceans with phosphorus drives the formation of harmful algal blooms and also results in substantial emissions of greenhouse gases such as methane. Many interventions exist. Go to phosphorusalliance.org to learn more. #Pweek #Pweek2026.
This is the pharaohs finger. From my Great Lakes fossil collection.

you don't have to be in the water to be exposed to toxins in cyanobacterial blooms: new work from Greg Boyer's group at the Great Lakes Center for Freshwaters and Human Health www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10....

Michigan and Ohio are both struggling to reduce the fertilizer runoff getting into Lake Erie which feeds cyanobacterial blooms, also called harmful algal blooms. Those toxic blooms can be hazardous to people and animals.

If you're in northern Michigan, drop by the U-M Biological Station in Pellston on July 30 for MISG Director Silvia Newell's talk about nitrogen as a driver of harmful algal blooms in the Great Lakes: buff.ly/V5tTtjA .bsky.social

(2/2) This project is aiming to fill sizeable gaps in our understanding of how Great Lakes nutrient cycles, food webs, and other conditions change during the winter. This is crucial for keeping humans and habitats safe from algal blooms, climate impacts, food web collapse, and other challenges.

In related news: "Michigan and its neighbors have missed a 2025 deadline to curb the farm pollution that feeds toxic algal blooms in western Lake Erie, despite 10 years of work and millions of dollars spent on the effort."

"When regulation comes, which it must if toxic algal blooms continue to thrive on the waterfront in Toledo, northwest Ohio farmers may regret allowing the Republican lawmakers they’ve given the large majority of their votes to ignore the public safety issue of polluted Lake Erie drinking water."

"If not for nutrient reduction efforts — including H2Ohio — over the past 10 years, blooms in Lake Erie would probably have continued to get worse. A rating of ‘unchanging’ is an improvement over what would have been ‘deteriorating.’”

A summer sampling blitz called the "HABs Grab" is helping scientists get a clearer picture of water quality and harmful algal blooms in multiple spots around the Great Lakes, with funding support from Michigan Sea Grant: buff.ly/HiES3uo .bsky.social .bsky.social

Spotlight on Lyndsie Collis, IAGLR Scholarship recipient! Studying how zooplankton interact with harmful algal blooms in western Lake Erie, this work sheds light on key processes shaping Great Lakes ecosystems.

How do harmful algal blooms reshape the Lake Erie ecosystem? Join us Wed 3/18 for .bsky.social dissertation presentation on how Microcystis blooms affect walleye reproduction, amphibian development, and fish communities. See flyer for details!

Yay my people! I have been looking for this starter pack! (if you're wondering who this weirdo who just followed you is, I study harmful cyanobacteria blooms primarily in W Lake Erie - Coop. Inst. of Great Lakes Research @ UMich + NOAA Great Lakes Env. Research Lab in Ann Arbor MI)

Could the air near Lake Erie be making people sick? My colleagues David Kennedy, Steven Haller, and others are studying effects of aerosolized algal toxins as part of our Great Lakes Center for Freshwaters and Human Health: www.13abc.com/2026/04/10/u...

Yes - especially here in western Lake Erie! Although I suspect multiple stressors (invasive quagga mussels, changing weather/climate) contribute to ecosystem shifts towards recurring harmful algal blooms as well as excess nutrients. Just my thoughts!

Harmful algal blooms (HABs) in the Laurentian Great Lakes can also produce toxic aerosols with wind and wave action. More research into the public health aspects of HABs are being done, but the message is clear: stay away from HABs - for your health!

What are harmful algal blooms? How do they affect our health? How should I report an exposure? Where can health care providers get more information on what to do? Our Great Lakes Center for Freshwaters and Human Health (NSF/NIH) provides answers with these two short informational videos 👇

A recent study in the Journal of Great Lakes Research reveals how climate change drives the rise of harmful algal blooms in Lake Superior’s nearshore. Despite its relatively undeveloped surroundings, the lake is warming faster than the other Great Lakes, a factor contributing to these blooms.
How do toxic algal blooms in our Great Lakes “make America Great Again” for those of us who drink something called water?

Harmful algal blooms form scums at the water surface and produce toxins that can harm ecosystems, water quality and human health. They have been reported in all 50 U.S. states, all five Great Lakes and nearly every country around the world.

Dr. Overpeck pointed to growing challenges such as heavier rainfall, increased flooding, algal blooms, and coastal erosion. “Our Great Lakes are becoming less great,” he said. “We’re getting these harmful algal blooms… more coastal erosion.”

What kind of #greatlakessci am I up to today? I'm looking for diel patterns in Lake Erie harmful algal blooms! In July 2023 we collected water quality + phytoplankton + Microcystis genomic samples 4x/day for 11 days. We collected over 4,000 samples. cool results available at some point!

The bacteria behind Lake Erie toxic plumes is more commonly found in warmer temps - not boding well with climate change. And Michigan has only met our 2015 goal with Ohio to reduce the nutrient runoff that grows algal blooms once. In a year with little rain. www.fox2detroit.com/news/um-rese...
